fact: October 17 is exactly 290 days into the year, which means there are only 75 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, squirrels are typically frantically burying nuts around this time - they can bury up to 10,000 acorns in a single autumn!
The sun is setting almost 2 minutes earlier each day around October 17 in most places, making bedtime feel like it comes super fast.
Maple trees are usually pumping sugar down to their roots right now, which is why their leaves turn those crazy bright red and orange colors.
October 17 falls in the middle of Libra season, when the zodiac scales are supposed to be perfectly balanced - just like how day and night were almost equal a few weeks ago!
Woolly bear caterpillars are often crawling everywhere around this date in North America, looking for cozy spots to hibernate through winter.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, October 17 is typically prime baby animal season - lambs, calves, and joeys are being born everywhere!
Spider webs covered in morning dew become super visible around this time because the autumn humidity is just right to make them sparkle like diamonds.
The moon often looks extra huge and orange when it rises around October 17 because of all the dust and pollen floating in the autumn air.
Geese flying south make their loudest honking sounds in mid-October - they're basically yelling directions at each other for their 3,000-mile journey!
October 17 is roughly when the first frost typically hits many northern areas, turning morning grass into crunchy white crystals that melt by lunch.
